titleStmt.title: Letter from William Upton Tyrrell to William J.H. Tyrrell 9, July, 1917
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: William Upton Tyrrell

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: William Upton Tyrrell has moved to less comfortable accommodation. He saw King George and Prince Edward drive through a nearby village.

msIdentifier.repository: OPW-Maynooth University Archive & Research Centre at Castletown

correspAction.placeName: Ballindoolin House, Ballindoolin, Edenderry, Kings Co., Ireland

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
9th July 1917. My dearest Daddy, I was delighted to get your grand
long letter yesterday. and to hear
all the news. It is a pity the meadows are
so bad but that is of course on
account of the dry weather. M Round
here there is not a single thing
except crops and all look very well. But I do not know how they are to
be saved as there is not a man in the
country. All women but they spend
their days working in the fields. The
people are all here exactly as usual
and you would not know there was
a war on except that the men are all
away. We saw George + Edward
the day before yesterday. They passed
through the village in a motor but did not stop. However they slowed down and we had a great look at them. I think it was a pity they did not stop for a minute just for a drink or something.
We are out of our good billets but
only moved about three miles The billets here are rotten so I am
living in a tent. It is clean
and very comfortable whereas the
houses are filthy. Why do they
put all villages down in a hollow
here? It is always the same tho! I meant to write to you
yesterday but had not time as we
only got two hours notice to
move. However luckily they did
not decide to move us in
the middle of the night or anything
silly. Bobby seems to be getting
on A1. Anyway his letters are
much more easily read than they
used to be. His way of spelling
church always amuses me. We may leave here in aboo
about a week but of course that
is only supposition. We know
Absolutely nothing! We had a
great thunderstorm on Saturday night
sometime in the middle of the night. It was just like the night the big
tree at the hall door was split. The lightning was so constant that
I am sure you could read the
newspaper. There was hardly one
second darkness all the time. I would not wonder if we soon had
another as the wether feels like it. Not a word of news so I must
shut up with very best love to all
from Your most affec Son
Willie Next parcel a few envelopes please
The chicken in the last parcel was
most excellent. I eat it last night
